cards.front: What is the double entry system?
cards.back: Every transaction affects at least two accounts. Total debits must always equal total credits, keeping the accounting equation balanced.
The left side of an account. Debits increase assets, expenses, and dividends; debits decrease liabilities, equity, and revenue.
The right side of an account. Credits increase liabilities, equity, and revenue; credits decrease assets, expenses, and dividends.
The side (debit or credit) where an account naturally carries its balance. Assets, expenses, and dividends have debit normal balances. Liabilities, equity, and revenue have credit normal balances.
Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Every transaction must keep this equation balanced.
A resource owned by the business that is expected to provide economic benefits in the future (e.g., cash, inventory, equipment).
An obligation to transfer economic resources to another party in the future (e.g., accounts payable, loans payable).
The residual interest in assets after deducting liabilities. It represents the owners' stake in the business.
Income earned by the business from selling products or providing services. Revenue increases equity and has a credit normal balance.
A cost incurred to generate revenue. Expenses decrease equity and have a debit normal balance.
A chronological record of a transaction showing the accounts debited and credited, amounts, and explanation. Journal entries are the starting point of the accounting cycle.
A list of all accounts and their balances to verify that total debits equal total credits. It is a control mechanism to detect errors in recording or posting.
